speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This fourth interview with Arthur Sundberg is recorded at his home in Potlatch on August 1st, 1975.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The first thing that I was thinking of asking you about was the question of working in the mill. You said to me once that it was an easy hire, easy fire policy. Yeah, each department in the mill, that is, the whole mill complex, it was divided up into quite a number of different departments. And, uh, I had a foreman in each department, and that foreman, he hired his own crew and he fired his own crew. That's the way it used to be. And, uh, there wasn't any unions or there was no recourse. Of course, if you was fired, you was fired, and that's it. Nobody ever thought about going on up, you know, to top management to complain or anything because that was, I never even heard tell of such a thing as that, you know. They just took it as a matter of course, you know. They'd hire you right on the spot without any interviews. They never, well, it was customary for a person, particularly a young person, asking for a job, foreman to kind of aim up and down and see how big he was and how strong he appeared to be. And, uh, if it was a job working with machinery, the foreman would probably say, well, that guy right over there, you go over there and he'll show you what to do. So, you'd go over there and that fellow, he would, you'd tell that guy, well, the boss sent me over here, you'd tell me, you're supposed to show me what to do. So, he'd probably handle a few boards and you'd do it like this and probably let you try it there for a minute or two and it looked like you could do it. But he'd just walk off. That was your breaking in period, see, and it was just up to you to do it. And, uh, there was undoubtedly some, you know, that had previous experience or at least had previous experience of being in the department even though they didn't work there. Because they didn't have any restrictions on visitors, you know, everybody and anybody, kids or anybody else could just come and go in around the mill. Nobody ever asked them any questions as to what they were doing there. So, consequently, kids that lived in that, around that industry, they got quite a bit of first -hand knowledge of what it was all about, you know, as they were growing up. I know kids would be seven, eight years old or probably younger than that, you know, running all over the plant down there. Kids 10, 11 years old are running all over the logs on the log pond and, you know, just trying to stump one another, running on the logs. So, the kids got quite a background knowledge of the industry, you know, before they went to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people would be in the average -sized crew? How many would the foreman be in charge of?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that would depend a whole lot on what department it was. Now then, you take, for instance, you'd start in, you know, well, the log pond had probably four or five men. Then when you get up into the sawmill, they probably had, oh, probably 40 or 50 men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For a single foreman?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. And then get out on the green chain, why, oh, there would be probably 20 men there, 10 men on each side of this chain pulling lumber, approximately that. Well then, in the days before they had the dry kill, the lumber used to go from the green chain directly to the lumber yard on these narrow gauge railroad cars. Well, they had, let's see,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think they had eight electric locomotives. And these locomotives would haul, the green lumber, they would haul about six or eight big units of lumber at a time. They were coupled together by two and a half inch extra heavy pipe. And then had a flat coupler on the end. And each one of these cars had a cast iron or cast steel draw head on each end of the car. And they would put this pipe into that draw head and then the man had to drop a pin in that hole. Well, these bars were 20 feet long, so they were pretty heavy. Well, in making up a train, there was a motorman and a switchman on each one of the locomotives. And they carried these bars on the side of the locomotive on hangers.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they would have to take one of these bars, each one of them, and they'd go down along the string of cars that had to be pulled out. And they would stick one end of the bar into this coupler and drop a pin in it. And then the other end laid on the ground and they would do that for each car as many as they were going to haul. Well, then the motorman, the motor operator, he would move ahead a little bit and then the switchman, he would have to lay on the side of the track with this other end of this bar on his leg. And he would have to gauge that so that it would couple into this draw head on this car as he backed up on it. And then he'd drop the pin in and jump out and go to the next one. And that's the way they made up their train. Well, then they would take it out to the lumberyard. And, oh,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: at that time, in the early days, there was about 26 or 28 alleys in the lumberyard. After that, it was made larger, but to begin with. And then there was double tracks in each alley. And all these alleys were about 1 ,800 feet long or something like that. Well, then this motor crew then, if, say, this tail end unit lumber, it had to go into alley 4 east. Well, they would throw the switch and they'd shove it into alley 4 east and uncouple it and take the bar out and hang the bar back on the Loki. Maybe the next unit had to go to alley 6 west, so they'd have to switch over and go down into alley 6 west and drop it off. Maybe there'd be two of them to drop off. And that's the way they used to distribute them. And then it got into that section of the alley. Well, then they had a man out there with a horse, and he would hook his chain onto that car, and he would pull that down and spot it in front of this pile where it was supposed to be piled. And then the piling crew, they might have to just push it by hand one way or the other, just get it spotted right. And then they would pile it. Well, then the, I think there was about five locomotives that were pulling green lumber. And there was three, I think it was, that were pulling dry lumber. But these locomotives that pulled the dry lumber in were, where they would haul six or eight units of green lumber, the same size locomotive, hauling dry lumber. They would haul 11, 12, maybe 14 units of dry lumber back in. Well, what they done then, when they brought it in, is they would pull it up behind the planer. And there was several tracks, there's a regular switch yard there. And they would just uncouple all of their units right there and stack their bars up on the Loki and then go back for another drag. But, like I told you, the planer, it had 22 machines in the planer. And there was a track going into each machine.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then there was two tracks going out from each machine. One track was for the lumber that was on grade for that particular order. And the other track was for what they called outs. You know, that was lumber that was either higher or lower grade than what was called for. And then that unit would be pulled out and go to the lumber shed for stacking up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the lumber went through the planer then, just when it was on order?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, that's right. It all stayed in the lumber yard rough, except, as I say, the outs. That went to the lumber shed and, of course, it had an order for certain species and certain grade and dimension. But then, whatever they could of that order, they filled that out of the shed that was already surfaced. And then the remainder come from the lumber yard to be surfaced.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, oh, in the early years, they didn't do any remanufacturing. The only remanufacturing that was done with the surface lumber was that this lumber that came into the lumber shed for stacking up... Well, it was all graded again in the shed. And then it was stacked in different units in this shed, you know, for dimension and grade and so on. But, if they had a, say, a number three board, and it was a number three because it had a two -foot crack on one end of the board, you know, a check. That made it a number three board. Or otherwise, if that wasn't there, it could be a number one board. Well, maybe the man that was grading in the shed, each one of those graders in there had a handsaw. And he would saw off two feet off on the end of it by hand, see, and then stack it up. That would upgrade the board. But that's the only remanufacturing that was done in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that the planer mill surfaced the lumber, what do you mean by that?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it was rough lumber when it come in there. When it come out, it was smooth lumber. You know, it was plain top and bottom and two edges, possibly. And a lot of the lumber in the early days was just, it was specified as two -side. Well, that meant that it was surfaced on top and bottom. And then, of course, you had other orders that was specified as four -side. That was top and bottom and the two edges were surfaced. But there was an awful lot of lumber shipped in the early years where the edges were never surfaced at all. Because, I think, the fact that there was a lot of that lumber that the purchaser would remanufacture it himself. And he would smooth up the edges. But, anyway, there was, oh, I'd say that it'd be 50 % of it or maybe more that was shipped just surfaced two sides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the relation between the mill and the WI&M railroad as far as the transference of the finished lumber to the railroad for shipment and out? Was it really run as a separate operation?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was, but it was entirely owned by the lumber company. But then it was run as a separate industry. And they had their own method of hiring and operations. And, of course, they had to comply with the Interstate Commerce Commission rules on everything. And so, but it was owned entirely by the lumber company. But then it was run as an entirely separate industry, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, and these foremen, if you wanted a job in the mill, you would go down there and speak to the foremen in the area where you thought you might want to work?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's right. That's right. That's right. You would go to any, whatever department that you thought that you would like to work. Now, for instance, out in the lumber yard, they had a foreman for the green lumber pilers. And then they had another foreman for the dry, for the men that loaded dry lumber, loaded out. And then they had another foreman for the pickup. Now, that pickup was, it was probably, oh, anywhere from two or three to five or six men in that pickup crew. But they had a lead man that was, you know, called a foreman. And what they'd done was any lumber that was, had fallen off or was scattered or, and then all these cross pieces that was in the pile, you know, that each course of lumber that they stacked, it had these one by four cross pieces on there so it would make air circulation through the stack. Well, this dry lumber crew, it was loading out. Why, that was so, oh, shortly after, it wasn't too long after the mill started, where that went on piecework on the chippel, you see. And they got so much, a thousand for loading it out. So what they would do, they'd just throw them one by fours off the pile to get them out of the road, see. Well, this pickup crew, they would go up and down the alleys and see that those were all put back, put up to where they wasn't laying on the rails. And so, you know, it was just kind of a housekeeping tour. Well, that was another little department, see. Well, a man might go out in the lumber yard and ask the dry lumber foreman for a job. And he'd say, well, right now I'm filled up. You know, I just can't hire anybody right now. But, old Carl over there, he'd probably go over and see him. Maybe he needs a man. So he'd go over and see old Carl. He was upon the pickup crew. And, yeah, I can put you on. You can start in right away. And you'd work at that for a while. But then after, maybe in a short while, it'd be an opening in this, where you wanted to start in the first place, in this dry lumber crew. So then you would, they needed a man there, so then you would go over there and go to work, see. Well, then old Carl, he'd hire another man in his pickup crew. But each one, they hired their own crew, and as I say, if it was necessary, they would fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it too hard to move from job to job once you were working in the mill?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: If you saw... No, I don't think so. Of course, you had to go and talk to that other foreman, you know, wherever you wanted to go.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And he'd say, well, where you working now?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'm working up in the sawmill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, what's the matter with that?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He'd say, well, it's just too noisy. I just want to get out of there, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And he might then, the first opportunity, ask the sawmill foreman about it and say, well, so and so asked me for a job.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: What do you think?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, go ahead and hire him. He's no good here anyway, and that's the way it goes, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then he wouldn't hire him, would he?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, but he might be good in that other one because that's where he wanted to be, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was a far cry from the way it's operated nowadays, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Nowadays, you have to first go to the employment office, and there you have to be interviewed as to past experience and all that. And then they might give you an aptitude test as to if they think that you can put a round peg in a square hole or a square peg in a round hole. And then, of course, now it's very seldom that you can go directly into a department or a division of the plant that you would like to go into right at the beginning. Because a big percentage of the new hires nowadays goes on an extra board list. And you're called out to day shift or night shift, and maybe one day you'll work in the power plant, and the next day maybe they'll call you out to work in the plating mill. So if you stay with it, you finally get to where you want to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would fellas want to move around very much in the old days, or would a guy get into the state in three years?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: In fact, the matter is, you take the average person working in the lumber industry, my opinion is it was just a matter of a job, a matter of making a living for himself and his family, if he had one. And they would go through an awful lot of hardships just in order to keep their job and be able to provide a living. More so than anybody nowadays, you take anyone your age, I don't think that the person could paint a picture and really get the idea across as to what people actually had to go through. And I say had to go through, that's probably a good way to put it, but that was just the way things were done, and if she was going to survive, you had to do it, that is all. Now,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: you take,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: nowadays, you see these young people, young men, I know I used to get so disgusted with some of them, they'd come down to work in the morning and they'd have on a pair of jeans. No underwear, they'd probably just a t -shirt and shorts, and a little low pair of socks and a pair of oxfords, and they'd come down there and it'd be below zero. And there they'd stand and shiver and shake like that. Well, you know, no man can work dressed like that. You have to be comfortable if you're going to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, you know, in my crew, of course, my crew was maintenance and construction, but I had men in my crew that would come down dressed that way, and I used to tie into them and ask them, why in the world didn't you buy some clothes?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And they'd mumble something, but then I always kept a lot of my clothes there, you know, I had clothes enough there for half a dozen men of my own. You know, sheepskin, overcoats, and all kinds of rain gear, pants and coats and overshoes and everything else. Well, gee whiz, them guys were wearing my clothes all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what about back then? Were you talking about how what they had to put up with?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Back then they used to dress, they knew they had to, and if they didn't, they'd just... You know, a foreman, if a fellow had the audacity to come down on the job in the early days like they do now, you know, dressed the way they do now, why, sure, the foreman wouldn't give one look if he was already employed, but he'd can him right on the spot. They wouldn't put up with anything like that. Them days you take in the cold weather,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: there was, oh, I don't know, there was any number of different combinations of footwear that the men used to wear, trying to keep their feet reasonably warm.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But there was a lot of them that wore these, what they called German socks. Well, they were just a felt boot, you know, they were not so thick. And they'd have on wool socks and then they'd have this thing on and then they'd have overshoes over the top of that. But, I guess personally I've tried every kind of a combination that's ever been thought of for the cold weather, and the best thing that I ever found was just a pair of low rubbers. Of course, they used to make them special for that, they don't make them anymore like that, but they were heavy, you know, but pliable. And then have, oh, maybe one or two felt liners, you know, insoles in there, see. And then maybe a pair of light socks and then a pair of heavy wool socks on that. Well, that was pliable and flexible, you see. Anything that's stiff on your feet, you'd freeze your feet, you know, because your feet wouldn't have a chance to flex. And then the same with your hands. I don't know whether I was probably among the minority, but I figured it out for myself a long time ago. It was customary for men to wear a pair of wool mittens, and those wool mittens would be inside of a pair of leather mittens. Well, I found out for myself, and I put a good many other men wise to it too, and they followed my theory, was that you could freeze your hands in that combination, because inside of this wool mitten, you had skin against skin on your fingers there, see, where you could just wear a cotton glove inside of that wool mitten and stay warm because you had insulation between your fingers, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it is. But even right now, you go around in the wintertime and you'll see these guys, well, they got to get their hands warm, so they go and buy a pair of wool gloves and a pair of leather gloves. You know, they've got to have a leather glove or mitten to handle lumber with, you know. But their hands are cold, and it's just because your skin is coming in contact with the skin on the other finger, you see, where if you had some insulation between those fingers, well, then you would stay warm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How tough was it for these, for the guys in the crews with Foreman?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Were the Foreman, I've heard about brown nosing, for example. Well, that's true. There was, you would probably find in some crews, you know, might be one or two in the crew that there's always brown nosing around the boss, you know, trying to win favors, you know. They would, oh, every chance they got, they'd slip around to the boss and, oh, John Doe, well, there he'd done this or he'd done that, see. Trying to get that guy in trouble didn't make me a little higher in the steam of the eyes of the boss. But that wasn't, I wouldn't say that that was the prevailing thing. It is, you know, you'd find that maybe with some, and then some it wouldn't work at all, you know. Maybe there was some Foreman that just wouldn't put up with that kind of stuff. If there was any investigations as to anything that wasn't supposed to be, well, he figured out he'd find that out for himself. But then there was some Foreman that, well, they lacked the, the intelligence and the experience of a Foreman, so they relied on some of these other guys bringing stuff to them, you see. You know, if a person doesn't have the qualifications for a Foreman, well, then he might have to resort to some of that stuff, see. And then there was some Foreman that'd done an awful lot of brown nosing to the superintendent, too, you know. And, of course, that's as far up as they could go. They never got it up to the management, top management, but then the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that mean making themselves look good at the expense of the crew?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: you know, if that had... that would keep snowballing, pretty soon he'd be the superintendent, wouldn't he?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Because he'd be running the whole show. And there was men that had those ambitions. Of course, most of them was kind of picked in the bud. They didn't get too far, but they did, they did, oh, multiply a little bit, but they always stopped it before it got too far along, see. But if a man had a crew here, maybe of ten men, well, maybe he was capable of combining his crew with another crew and making it all in one. You know, there were some people that had the capability of doing that. And then there were probably some, too, that wanted to do that, and maybe they had an opportunity to do it. And then after they got it, they found out they couldn't handle it. Well, then they would get one of their subordinates to take over part of it, you see, part of the load. Oh, there were lots of different combinations, you know, that couldn't be worked out. Sounds like there was a fair degree of flexibility there in the set -up to permit that kind of thing. If you could have, actually, let's say, subdivide your crew, if you're the foreman and have one of your men run part of the crew, it sounds like it wasn't too highly structured, is what I'm saying. Well, that's true, but of course this subordinate to the foreman, he didn't get any recognition of being a leader or anything else. That was just between him and his foreman, you know. And he didn't get any extra pay. It was just kind of making him look good to his boss. That was all. It was just a glory thing, you know. He didn't get any extra compensation for it or anything of the kind. Well, I've heard it said that whether or not you had a good foreman was what made all the difference for you working. Well, that's very true. They did have some awful good foremen, and where they did, why, the only way that you could ever find an opening in that particular department would be that somebody would die. You know, it was a lifetime job for a lot of men. You know, they worked in one department their entire life, and that was it. They didn't have any desire to work anywhere else because they liked... John Doe was a foreman, and they got along good, and he was fair, and he never asked anything of any of his men that was unreasonable, and consequently they liked the work there, so they wanted to stay there. There was a lot of that, you know. It wasn't all this tooth and claw theory at all, you know. There was a lot of contentment, although they worked hard.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you know, the average man that worked in the industry in the earlier days,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: the time he got his day's work in and he got home and he got his supper, he would probably sit down and read the newspaper for a few minutes and then pile in the bed, because he had her for that day because he was tired. He was tired right down to the bone. And then he would get up the next morning and do it all over again. And that was the routine of the average. And I don't think they complained about it too much because they didn't know anything different. It's the only thing they ever seen and it's the only thing they ever knew. That's the way their dad lived and that's the way their granddad lived. You know, it was sun up till dark and pile in the bed and the next morning start all over again. Most of these folks who were just working in the mill like that, I know I've asked you something like this before, but this makes me think about that again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of them make money while they were doing this? Were they saving?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Very, very, very few. They had to be very frugal and the man and his wife had to work in very, very close harmony in order to save any money. For the average person in the industry, it was just a hand -to -mouth existence and that's the reason that they just couldn't afford to take any time off. And it was just almost entirely unheard of for anybody outside of, get up into the upper echelons of the industry or anybody ever taking a vacation. It was six days a week, ten hours a day. There were some men that had jobs that was seven days a week, twelve hours a day. And they just seemed to like it. It was just a way of life and they just keep going that way. It was twelve hours a day and seven days a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people have ambition to become foremen?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course I don't know the percentage. I think the average person, if the opportunity was offered to him, he would probably like to at least try it because of the fact that he probably would get away from the hard work part of it. Because a foreman, he would be just patrolling in his department and seeing that everything was clicking the way it should be.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then of course he had to be a man that would make some decisions.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: For instance, now the crew is loading out dry lumber in the lumber yard. They had just a little cubicle of a shack here at the center of the lumber yard. And there was a man that his job was to write out tickets for these units of lumber. And they were all written out in duplicate.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, this hard sheet, I think I explained that to you once before, that they used on the boxcars where they loaded out. This hard sheet would come out to this man in the lumber yard. And this called for 20 ,000 feet of a certain size and a certain grade in a certain species of lumber, you see. And then it would maybe be another 10 ,000 feet of something else. Well, you could only load out about 2 ,500 feet on one of these cars. So consequently he would have to write out several tickets, you know, because there was one copy. That ticket had to go with the car, with the unit, into the planer. And, well, I said it was made in duplicate. I think it was made in about four copies because the crew that loaded it out, they would keep one copy. And then they would stick the rest of them between two boards in the load. See, they'd raise the board up and lay the tickets down and lay this on top. And then they'd indicate where the ticket was by either a stick or maybe if they had a crayon they'd make a mark there. Then when it got into the planer, they would keep one ticket. And then one ticket would have to go to the car where they loaded it. And of course these tickets all had to coincide, you know, as to... That way they kept track of what was going to the car, although the checker on the dock would... he would have to count the pieces in each load and put the exact scale down on this hard sheet as to what it was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, uh... that's the way they handled that part of it, you know. But then the foreman, he had to make a decision sometimes because you and I was a crew, two men and a crew, stacking out lumber. So we'd go in there and this fella in there, he would hand us a whole stack of tickets, maybe enough to run us for the entire day. And so we'd look them over and this called for a certain specie and grade. And it would... this man in this little house there, he would have to put down the pile number because every pile in the lumber yard was numbered. So you would go there to this pile, that number that was indicated, and when you got there, why, maybe there wasn't enough lumber left in that pile to make it fill out that unit. So, you'd see the foreman going by, you'd holler at him, he'd come over and say, well here, we've got this ticket here for so much and there isn't enough in this pile there. So, he would give you authority to go to another pile of the same specie and grade and so on, you know. And then it was up to him then to go back and make this correction known to this man that was writing out the tickets, you see. And he had to make decisions, but they usually were small decisions, you know, there wasn't anything big. And then of course sometimes too, he had to intervene with... you take where crews were gyppoing, it was just dog -eat -dog proposition because, for instance, now these little railroad cars, they were quite heavy. They were made out of heavy timber and heavy cast iron wheels and all that, but they didn't have any power of their own. So, if you and I were, say, loading out, we had ten tickets here, we had to have ten of those cars.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, we would go any place where we could find an empty car standing and grab that car, but if that car was in an alley and it had a stick stuck into it to indicate that it was really taken, you couldn't touch it. So, if you did, you got in bad. So, you'd probably have to go clear back to the loading dock where they was emptying these cars and you'd have to push them by hand clear out to the lumberyard. And these guys were eager beavers, you know, they would... were... two of those cars would probably be a good load for two men. They would probably take six or eight, you know, bump them right together and push the whole string up. Well, they had one man on the plant. It was his job to go around and oil these cars, you know, the journals on these cars.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, undoubtedly, he would miss some of them, you know, and sometimes those journals got pretty dry and they moved awful hard. But, you know, sometimes they'd get into a squabble over some of them being hog -ish and they would say there's ten empty cars on the dock and there was three crews looking for cars. One crew tried to grab them all, then there'd be a squabble, see. You had... supposed to share. Well, then it was probably up to the foreman to make a settlement there, you know. He'd have to intervene and... Because that's where it goes when they're gyppoing, you know, they get pretty eager.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, we lived, as you're coming into town, you know, there's a row of houses out there on the flat there just opposite the lumberyard. And we lived there for a while and in the summertime we could hear them boards just slapping down in that lumberyard there at three and four o 'clock in the morning. And you could hear them out there at nine o 'clock at night. But they was working gyppo and some of them men were really tough. So in order to make what they thought was a lot of money, they'd be out there at three o 'clock in the morning either loading or unloading lumber and you could hear them boards just slapping down as they throw them down, you know. And instead of working a ten hour day, why they'd probably work fourteen, sixteen hour days. There was no limit, they didn't care. If you went out there and worked a double shift there, that was fine. There was lumber there to handle it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the theory of gyppo?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I'm curious about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a practice that was instituted after the mill had been going for a while?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah. Yeah, but it was put in as an incentive. You know, a gyppo crew is bound to be more productive in the course of a day than a crew that's working by the hour. Because a crew that's working by the hour, it's only reasonable to assume that wherever possible they'll drag their feet, you know. You take even a man that's working with machinery, now like a man that's feeding a machine, putting the lumber into a machine, and then there's another man that has to take it away from the machine. Well now that machine has to, that has to keep running. But they would have extra crewmen there that would, relief men that were supposed to relieve a man on the job without stopping the machine. Well, you know, a reasonable time of relief would probably be 10 minutes or 15 minutes at the most. But if they were, you know, working by the hour, well, that 10 or 15 minutes could drag into 20 or 25 or 30 minutes and longer, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was just for a break?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, yeah, that was, you know, they would probably have to relieve themselves or, you know, go to the toilet or, and that was about the only excuse that you had to leave your post.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But some of them really took advantage of it because I figured, oh well, I might as well be restful because John Doe, he's running it while I'm gone. Well, there was one bad thing about that, and that was that maybe they had one relief man in the department and he was supposed to relieve that whole department and, you know, maybe 10 or 15 men in there. But if several of them took too much time, then the others didn't get any time, see. And it was up to the foreman to kind of watch some of those things and see that they didn't get clear out of hand. Well, when they went on gyppo work and they got paid with a thousand feet of lumber for, that they handled, well, then they got eager and they didn't want any time off. And when the noon hour come, they'd gulp down a sandwich or two and then they wouldn't wait, they wouldn't take off the full noon hour, but they'd just eat a bite and then go right back to work, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And starting time in the morning, well, instead of starting at 7 o 'clock, well, they'd be down there at 6 and get squared around and then they'd start to work. And then quitting whistle and blow at night, they'd say, well, we've only got one unit left to go to finish out this bunch of tickets, so let's just stay and finish that out, see. That was the incentive that built up with this piecework, you see. But then these eager beavers cut their own throat and the others too because when they got to figuring out the payroll, if the average pay was, say, $2 .50 a day, and here was some men that was making $5 a day, well, then they'd say, well, we're paying too much for this gyppo. So instead of increasing the gyppo, they would probably cut it a little bit. Well, then they had to work that much harder in order to make that $5.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Like I think I told you before that the time I loaded cars on the loading dock, we got 15 cents a thousand, and that was divided between two men. Well, now a thousand feet of lumber, it makes a pretty good -sized pile of lumber, and you had to push that into a boxcar and the man inside had to stack it up for 15 cents.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then, even there, loading cars, why, if lumber was run, waiting to be put in the car, there were some of those crews there that they'd put in several hours sometimes, overtime, you know. Well, then, making up the payroll right here was this crew here, look at how much money they're making. Well, they're paying them too much. You see, that's what would build up from that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it discouraged the guys who were working on hourly wage to an extent?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, no, no. No, it didn't. It didn't run a brush off on them, I don't think, unless they wanted to transfer from a job on an hourly wage to a job that was on gyppo. And if they wanted to gyppo, well then, the reason they wanted to gyppo was they figured that they was just man enough to earn more money. But you had to earn it, there was no other way of getting it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when they started gyppo in Potlatch?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were doing it by the time you were 17?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, no, well, yeah, they were. But then it was just... Of course, the gyppo, the only place they worked on gyppo was piling green lumber, loading out dry lumber, and loading the boxcars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were floating wet?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take the pond and sawmill and green chain and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's why they were sure to stay on board when you can't even walk this up and down?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they had part of the planer, they got that on gyppo, but it didn't stay as long as it did in these other departments because...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take... your earnings in the planer was pretty much controlled because you were assigned to one machine. That was your station, you see. And you couldn't earn any more than what lumber was assigned to that machine, you see. So there was really not much incentive there for gyppo.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then, of course, the power departments, you know, the power plant, you know, that was... They worked now. The fact of the matter is you take the fellas that worked in the power plant in the early years. They worked twelve hours a day and seven days a week.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, no, it was just common play. And then the night watchmen...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The night watchmen... Well, there was three different routes.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the one that... part of his route took him through the sawmill, he could get in there out of the weather and probably get warmed up for a few minutes, once every hour. And then the one that went... that took in the planer... And, of course, the dry sheds and part of the lumber yard... Once every hour he could duck into the powerhouse at the planer to get warmed up for a few minutes. But then the watchman that was out in the lumber yard, he didn't have any shelter or no place to go to get warm because he was right out in the open. You see, all three routes took in part of the lumber yard, but then there was one route that took in the far part, see, so he never had... And he had to make his rounds once every hour. And each one of them was long enough so that just at a slow walk it took you a full hour to make a turn. And, you think, in the wintertime when the snow was deep, that one in the lumber yard, he had a pretty rough go because he had to make his own trail, you know, the snow kept piling up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that was 12 hours a day and 7 days a week.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then the men that worked in the pump house, of course that was a nice job because it was warm in there, they run these pumps by steam and everything was nice and warm. And their job consisted mostly of just keeping everything polished and wiped off nice and clean, everything was painted up bright, you know, red. But then they had to be there to see that everything was functioning right because these pumps, they worked on off the governor. And as the pressure would drop in the water mains, by then these pumps would increase, you know, with their speed. But anyway, there were two men that worked in the pump house, one on the day shift and one on the night shift, 12 hours a day and 7 days a week.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And everybody was as happy as could be because they had a job, but nobody accumulated any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there ever cases that you can remember where the foreman was so disliked by his men, by his crew that they managed to get rid of him? Or that he just killed in the eyes of management because he couldn't?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was a good many cases, there was a few cases right here, but you take throughout the industry there was ever so many cases where the crew, someone in the crew would actually just beat the tar out of the foreman physically, black his eyes and beat him up so he couldn't walk.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And some of these foremen were disliked just that much. And you take, I don't know whether you, in some of your interviews on the lumbering here, if you,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: probably there's some mention made about Charlie Peterson that was the superintendent here for years. Well, when I first knew him, he was the yard foreman.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And he was one of these brown -nosed eager beavers and he got to be superintendent. But there was more than once where he would wear black glasses for a week or two at a time because cover up these black eyes or somebody just beat the tar out of him. And I know there was for a long time there that he used to patrol the lumberyard there on the saddle horse. Because some guy would take after him while he'd get out running with the saddle horse, get out of his way. Well that's just how much they thought of him. But, anyway, he got up to be superintendent, that is a shipping superintendent. Of course he was the superintendent of everything outside of the sawmill. That is he was the superintendent of all the lumberyard and the planter and the shipping dock and all that. And he was the superintendent of all the lumberyard and the shipping dock and all that. And right at the last of his career was they moved him down to Lewiston as a consultant and he was a big shot down at the Lewiston plant. So as far as the company was concerned he really worked his way up in the eyes of the company. But as far as his education was concerned I know that I went to school with his oldest boy. He had two boys and it was common knowledge that these kids were teaching their dad how to read and write. But still he got to be the superintendent. And it was mostly just brown nose that got him there but then he was successful at doing it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I would think that if you couldn't get along too well with the men or if the men didn't respect him that would make it a lot more difficult. That didn't have anything to do as far as management was concerned. They didn't operate a corporation a long time ago like they do now. Now you know why this... any man in any branches of management is really screened as to his capabilities. And industry nowadays, every lead man, every foreman, every superintendent and every manager goes through a continual process of education and re -education. As far as getting along with people and being able to handle people and all that sort of thing. That's something that was never even dreamt of years ago. It didn't matter as far as the company was concerned. It didn't matter whether or not the qualities they were looking for in a foreman or superintendent were not necessarily ability to get along with the men. No, it was production.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: If they could point to the production sheet and say, look here now, we averaged 200 ,000 feet a day through this department at a cost of so many dollars. That was it. That never went any further than that. That was it. They didn't care whether John Doe didn't like the foreman or anything else. That didn't enter into it at all. It was so much production for so much cost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the foreman have a lot of directives, a lot of instructions on how to operate from the superintendents?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It might be private. It might be behind a lumber pile, but then it is still being private. It's hard to say what their conversations were all about. They might be talking about the score of the last Sunday's baseball game as far as I'm concerned.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: If a man was assigned a job as a foreman of a crew, then it was up to him to see that that crew turned out so much work and handled so much lumber.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: If he couldn't do it, then they would move him out and put somebody else in as foreman. When it got down to the foreman, in most cases, they got along pretty good because actually the foreman was just one of the crew.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Personalities didn't change very much. He was probably chosen to be a foreman because he showed some ability to lead and to carry on this particular job.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Pay -wise, they would probably be two and a half cents an hour or five cents an hour at the very most. The difference between what the foreman got and what the man and his crew got. It was mostly just, you wasn't doing the physical labor probably that your crew was doing, but you didn't get much extra pay either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the foreman were under much more pressure than the crew as far as the pressures?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The foreman, he was right in the middle between the superintendent that demanded these things and the men that had to perform these things. He was right there in the middle and he had to try to make both of these groups happy.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The foreman was always under a lot more pressure that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would most foremen mind if the guys wanted to sneak a smoke, get off work, have a little rest?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Some of them, in order to further their own interests, they wouldn't have to see it or catch a man at it. But if some brown nose would even come and tell him that John Doe was out there in the can smoking. Well, he'd probably write out his time and give him his time. And then maybe another foreman, under the same circumstances, he would say, now listen, I know you're smoking out there and you know it's against the rules.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you better watch it and be there and be sure that nobody catches you at it because if this is reported another time or two, the only thing I can do is let you go. He would try to reason with the man. So there's a difference in personalities in that respect.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you wouldn't find a foreman who is likely to just approve it, say, to the man that's okay?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was a lot of Bull Durham and Duke's mixture that was smoked. And you could smell that darn stuff for quite a ways. You take these toilets that they had around the plant, there was just a hole in the ground with a building set on top of it. And you sat down on a two -by -four and there was no partitions or no sign of any privacy at all. And then each one of them, these buildings, they had a cupola on top for a ventilator. But you could walk past one of those and you could probably be possibly in some cases up to a hundred feet away walking past. And you could smell this Bull Durham, you know. You know somebody was in there smoking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there often pretty good friendships between the foremen and the men?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, a lot of it. They had a few foremen around that got out a lot of production and still they got along so well with the men that the men would just actually literally lay their lives down on the line for them. They just liked their boss that much because he was just an awful nice fellow. This idea of a boss being a Simon LeGree, that was pretty much in the minority. And that wasn't really the thing that prevailed all over, but it did in some instances. But there was a lot of them that were just really good fellows. They were kind and considerate. And I think that it really paid off for them because the men didn't try to take advantage of them. Because they were considerate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that you got to become a foreman? How did that come about?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh no, I didn't ask for it. I think it was just something that just kind of... just a ball having to roll and stop right there or something. But I think that a man's got to show some qualifications and some ability to lead. Now then, you know, when you start out, you take in my case, and I suppose with many others, where a person would start out just as a lead man, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you probably have maybe one man working with you. And maybe even be several. But then anyway, the real foreman, or the superintendent, whatever that might be, would give the instructions to the lead man as to what he wanted. Then it was up to the lead man to understand exactly what he wanted and go ahead with it and put that theory over then to these men that were working with him. That made him a lead man, see. Well then, you know, you worked that way for a while, well then maybe something would come up to where there was some expansion. Then the lead man was made a foreman. And he had more responsibility, took in a bigger scope. Probably more men. And that's just the way it develops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that something like what happened in your case?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think so. At least that's the way I always felt about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What crew were you on when you became the lead man?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I was the steam fitter in the sawmill. And they had a big modernization program going on where they'd done a lot of new construction. And they had to make a lot of changes, you know, physical changes in the plant.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so being as I was a steam fitter,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well then there was a lot of, the fact of the matter is the whole sawmill was all completely repiped and changes put in. And they put in a big low pressure turbine for generating electricity.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so all this exhaust from all these different components in the sawmill that used steam. Take the shotgun that drove the carriages and all these steam cylinders, jump cylinders all over. They used the exhaust into the atmosphere. Well, all that exhaust had to be captured and put into a system that went to this turbine to generate electricity. So there was a big job come up, you know. And of course being as I was working as a steam fitter before this job come on, well then it was only natural that they put men with me, you know, to carry on some of this work. Well then it developed from that to bigger things and so on. The last job that I had done was to completely change the whole sawmill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you take all this wooden structure that supported the sawmill, we took that out and put in steel. We took out all the wooden floors on the bottom and put in fills and concrete floors and all steel columns and steel beams. And changed the machinery around and took a lot of the machinery out and put other machinery in and rebuilt the entire sawmill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then I retired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was the first form of job that you had? When did this first changeover from the exhaust?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well that was in 1924.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then I retired in 1964 so that was a period of about 40 years that I put in as an elite man and foreman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you say that your own attitude about the way to deal with the men working with you fit? What was that when you were a foreman and elite man? How did you look at it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I had the opportunity right up until the time I retired of pretty much choosing anybody that worked in my crew. Although they did come through the employment office but I could either take them or leave them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then every man that was hired in the latter years, he wasn't considered as a permanent employee until he put in 30 days.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So if something would show up in a man's work or his qualifications within that 30 days, well then you could turn him back to the employment office. But that's something that didn't happen very often except where we had a big job and had a lot of extra men.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I know we had to have some extra welders one time and so they sent some up from the plant at Lewiston to help us out. Well, there was one guy in particular that they sent up here that he was probably misinformed as to the job that we wanted him to do.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But all he wanted to do was stand there with a stinger in his hand. And then he worked it and it would be done while somebody else had to do it. And then when they got it all laid down, then he would weld it. Well that didn't work out in what we wanted. We wanted men that could lay out steel, cut it, fabricate it, put it in place and weld it. So I sent him back to Lewiston. I couldn't use him at all because I had other men that was doing what we wanted to do and I couldn't have one man just standing there as a kingpin with just all he'd done with a stinger.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then we, you know, for tearing out timbers and stuff, we had a lot of bullgang work. It was just men that were just grunt men, you know. Some of them were real good and then others wouldn't qualify at all because of the fact that they were afraid. Things around them scared them and they'd just stand there. And they wouldn't touch a thing unless she was right there to tell them, take a hold of that and lift on that, see. Or help them men lift that timber. And you couldn't do that all the time. So what you had to do, you had to just weed them out. You had to have men that would, if there was timbers to be moved, if there was three men to do it, well then all three of them had to do the equal part.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And those were some of the conditions, you know, that you would run into.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think of yourself as being tough?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, never. Never because I worked, you know, I was just one of the workmen for so darn long that I don't think that you could go through some of the old timebooks and find some of these fellows and ask about them. And I don't believe that any of them would tell you that I was tough because that's something that I never wanted to be.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I actually didn't, from the way you talk, I don't really think you would have been. I was feeling you'd be fairly understanding. Well, you know when you got men that are qualified to do the work, and they're journeymen, carpenters or welders or whatever it might be, well, you know, it just kind of falls into a routine there that every morning you would probably roll out some blueprints. And say, well now, this is it, we're going to do this today, this section right here. They'd understand what you're talking about and maybe there's some details there that they'd want to ask questions about. And during the day they'd want to come back and recheck on the blueprints and whatever the work might be and you just worked with them and explained to them what it was and everything was fine. They just went ahead with their work and everything was fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your experience with the higher ups when you were, just back in the earlier days, I'm thinking in the 20s?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I got along good with the lower echelons of the higher ups, but then the top brass I didn't get along with them at all.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: One reason for that was that, you know, going back to the days of the IWWs, you know, they were, I think possibly that they created a lot of difficulties, but then they were blamed for a lot of things too that they didn't, that they shouldn't have been blamed for. That was my personal belief.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The Wobblies, as they were called, of course what they were trying to do was to organize men. They started out in the logging camps, you know, and of course they never really got down around the lumber mills much. But anyway, it put enough scare in the top management of the lumber industry to where the lumber industry formed and developed a union. It was run by management, but it was supposed to be a union for the men, see, because what they wanted to do was to keep organized labor out. So this union that was developed was called the Four Hells. The Loyal Labors of Lumber... The Loyal Region of Loggers and Lumbermen. That's what it was. Anyway, the Four Hells. Well, that was just 100 % controlled by the management, you know, and there was some representative of the management that sat in on every meeting. And of course they tried to make the men think, you know, that they had a union, you know, and they was running things. Well, then of course the... I believe the Supreme Court made a ruling after a few years, and it might not have been the Supreme Court, but anyway there was a ruling made that this Four Hells was illegal. Because it was... it had members of management as part of their governing board. Well, then they tried to develop another kind of a lumber union, and they operated that for a few years where management didn't take an active role in the meetings, but they did from the outside. And finally, they had men that represented them, so it was really not much difference in the Four Hells. So finally they organized labor. They come in and they tried to organize. And of course this town here, this company, was a closed town. You see, the company owned all the houses, they owned everything. So they wouldn't rent them a hall, these labor organizers, they wouldn't rent them a hall to meet in. So they went over here to Onaway to an independent -owned hall, and they advertised that they was going to have some meetings over there. So just through curiosity why most of the guys working here would go over there, you know, and hear what them guys had to say. And of course I was one of them. Well, it finally got down to where they took a show of hands as to whether or not the men in this hall at that particular meeting thought that they should try to organize. And of course, the hands went up all over the place, you know, so it made them think, you know, that they wanted a union. Well, then they come down to the point where they was going to choose somebody to represent them to the management, you know, as an organized union. Well, then everybody hung back. Nobody wanted that job, see. They all wanted to join, but nobody wanted. And so, I don't know, some of them, they pointed their finger at me and I said, no, I don't want it. And we'll elect somebody else permanently. And I said, well, if that's what you want, why, okay. Get the thing started. So I was appointed to represent the organized labor, and it was up to me to go and confront the top man, you know. So I asked for an appointment, and I called the secretary and I asked for an appointment to see him. And of course, you know, the work got there long before I did, you know, because, you know, there were stool pigeons all over the place. And so, of course, he didn't have anything, he couldn't help himself. He had to grant this interview, you know, because the law was starting to get tough. So when I come into his office there, he was sitting there at this big desk, and boy, his face was just white. He was so darned mad, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so I talked to him and asked about wanting to rent a hall. So he couldn't refuse me because the law was on my side, see, in that respect. But, gosh, you know, he was just sitting there grittin' his teeth and he was just white. And so we got the thing started, and that's as far as I ever went. But from that day until the day that that man died, he hated my guts. But still, in all, I did raise up in job categories on the plant, but not because he wanted me to. It was just because of my own hard work and ability that put me up, not because he had anything to do with giving me any promotions at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that all you spoke to him about, was renting a hall?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's right, and then telling him that we were going to organize a union. But I was the fall guy right there. You know, somebody had to, if they was going to organize a union, somebody had to do it. But I was never elected to be the president of the union or nothing else. I was just appointed temporarily, see. But, boy, he hated my guts, I'll tell you. Because I was the one that brought the news to him that they were going to do that, and if I hadn't, somebody else would have eventually done it anyway, I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, gosh, I don't know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was about the time that they passed that Taft -Hartley law.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I can't tell you what year that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it before the Depression?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, it was after the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the AFL? Or what was the big overall union that was coming in?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The AFL is the one that started it, you know. There was men that represented the meat -cutters union in Spokane that come down here. And there was men that represented the steelworkers. And there was men from different groups of the AFL that come down here to organize this thing. And, boy, I'll tell you right now, when organized labor gets a bit in their teeth, they're going to do something. They're going to just about do it. Well, then, of course, they got this lumber union, IWA, something like that they call it. I don't know the name. But anyway,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It's affiliated with, it's strictly a lumber union, but it's affiliated with the...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I don't want to spend too long since I've had anything to do with any of them that I've forgotten what...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for them to organize at that time when they came in here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No. No, it wasn't. You know,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: some missionary evangelist can come in and set up a tent or rent a hall. And people come there just through curiosity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And before the night's over with, why, maybe that evangelist has converted to a hundred people, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then, maybe the next day, they've lost most of their conversion. And that's the way it is with organized labor. You know, they put up a few handbills here and there, and they get the word out they're going to have a meeting, and they want everybody to come out. Well, everybody's curious as to what they've got to say and what they've got to propose, so they come out and they tell them, you know, that... You've got to organize because if you don't, you're just going to work for these small wages the rest of your life. If you're ever going to get anywhere, you've got to organize. Well, sure, everybody will write their name down on their card and fine. It only costs you two dollars to join, and it only costs you a dollar and a half a month for dues, and, you know, nothing to it. Well, the average union member, all he contributes towards organized labor is paid dues. But it's these...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, the organizers and the officers of the union is the one that really does all of the work, and it gets all these raises, and it gets all these different benefits that men finally get. But the average union member, all he does is pay his dues. That's all he ever contributes to being a union member.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why you decided to take the ball in that case and go to that cheese?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, but I don't know. It's just... Some people are just born kind of cut out for doing things or moving things, you know. Somebody's got to be a prime mover on things, and it comes natural to some people. You take as far as being able to speak in front of an audience, that's something that never did bother me. I was always more or less of a good speaker in front of an audience.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, I don't know, it just kind of more or less comes natural.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you regret it after you saw the band?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, no, because I'm bullheaded too, you know, to a certain extent on some things, because once I make up my mind to do something, well then that's it. I won't back down from anyone. I'm still the same way, and I always will be.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But that was it though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After that you didn't have anything more to do with the organization?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, that was the end of that part of it. And of course, you know, then, of course, my status with the company was so that I couldn't be a member of the union. Because I was representing the company, see, as a foreman. You were a foreman at the time you went to see the... ...all those duties that a foreman would do. And as a representative of the company, of course, I wasn't eligible to belong to any union. So, consequently, I never did belong to that union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it, looking back at it, that it hurt you as far as advancement? Or did it hurt you and it didn't make any difference?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't know. I sometimes think that it did, and then other times possibly not.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I...you know, I worked on a salary for a long time. Oh, a few years. And then when that depression come along, well then, of course, they cut down on everything, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But they...a good percentage of the people that worked here, they was given enough work, you know, so as to pay their rent and their hospital fee and their lights and water and so on.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so, you know, as far as cost was concerned, the company cut down their cost right down to the bone. So, of course, I was taken off in the salary and I was put on hourly rate. And, of course, my earnings on the hourly rate was just, you know, it was nothing...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I had fairly consistent work, but the pay was...well, we only got 27 cents an hour. And probably four hours a day is all we got. Because they divided up what little work they had. They divided up as much as they could, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So, when the things got better and they got to running steady again, well, I wasn't put back on that salary. I was kept on the hourly rate, although I would periodically get an increase.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so I never questioned it very much. But then they come out and they called a meeting down here at the Lewis and Clark Hotel in Lewiston with the top management of Potlatch Forest. And they called in all the supervisory personnel of the company from all their units. And they discussed a plan that they had been thinking about and was thinking about putting into practice. And they said, well, let's put this as a pension plan. And the original setup was that a man would have to be on a supervisory job for ten years in order to qualify for this company pension.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this company pension would amount to about...let's see, what was it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think it was 60 percent, I believe it was, of his salary would be paid out as a pension. That was including Social Security. So, time went on and on and, of course, they had changed management here several different times. But then they changed the rules and they made the rules that they would pay this pension to just salaried supervisory personnel. Well, I was an hourly paid supervisor. And so the manager come to me and said, well now Artie said we've been thinking about your retirement whenever the time comes. I hadn't long wasted a go yet. But if we put you on a salary, you'll have to take a cut and pay. Because, he said, we don't pay any of our salaried men in your category as much money as we pay you. He said, you're the highest paid man in your category that we've got anywhere in our organization. And I said, well, I don't want any cut. Well, he said, that's just the way it is. But still, he said, you've got to kind of look ahead to when you do retire. But it wasn't put to me, you know, to where it was very clear on this thing. Well, anyway, then they changed managers. The new manager, he called me in and he said, we've been checking up on your status here on this pension.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And he said, it says a man has to be salaried for 10 years. But you've only got 9 and a half years to go to where you're retired.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And I said, well, it was about all these other years. I said, don't they qualify?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, according to the rules that they've set up, boy, he says they don't. So, it wasn't long after that until he left and they got another manager in. And then he said, well, he looked into my pension. And he said, don't worry for a minute. He said, we're going to get it for you. Well, they changed managers three times.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Four times. And every one of them was going to get it for me. But when I retired, I got the same pension as the run of the mill employees would get at that time. And it was $50 a month where I should have gotten at least $400. So, you see, it was just the, well, this started out with this manager that I told you hated my guts. It started out right there. And I blame him for just fixing it up so that it'd keep me off from it, you know. So, I'm losing,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: oh, I'm losing at least $400 every month and have been now for over 11 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he still in the corporation?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, he was out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the one that didn't like me? Yeah, he was out when they came up with their pension plan?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, he was here, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was here then?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you figured that there's a good chance that he had to do with taking the non -salary then?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: At least that's the way I always felt about it now. I could be wrong, but then that's the way I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there very few people in that class that you were in who were being paid an hourly wage?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I was about the only one.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That is, not only in this unit, but in the whole PFI organization. Everybody else had gone back on a salary. Yeah. Although, you take as long as I was on the job, I was making more money than any of them. Okay.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's really too bad. Well, we'll survive. But then, I think for the work that I'd done for the company...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And I don't think I was treated right in that respect. Because I think you can go right down to Lewiston and check with any of the brass down there. And ask what they think about my work. And I think I'd get a pretty good rating from any of them. Top man down.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I want to ask you about that IWW activity some. We touched on it a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's your understanding of what their aims were and what they did in the camps?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'll tell you, to start out with, I think their principal aim at the beginning was to get clean beds and decent food in the camps. You know, at the time they started, before that, every man had to carry his own bedroll. They had wooden bunks built in the camps. But then every man would get down at the barn and get an armful of straw, bring it up and throw it in that bunk and spread his own blankets on it, and that was his bed.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the food, of course I can't tell you this from first hand because I wasn't there. But from all the men that I've talked to, was that the camp foreman, if he could run his camp at the least cost, he would raise himself up in the esteem of the company. But if he would raise the cost by ordering real good expensive food for the men, well then he didn't stay there very long as a foreman because it would cost too much to run his camp. They would buy the very best Timothy hay and they bought the very best grade of oats, and they hired the very best blacksmith to shoe their horses, and they bought the very best harness and equipment for their horses. But when they come to the feet of the men, well, of course there wasn't any of the camps that ever had any refrigeration, but nevertheless I know that there was meat that left this store down here. They wholesaled, you know, for all the camps. The meat was shipped out of here that actually maggots were crawling out of it, and they didn't want to cut it up for their meat market here so they shipped it to the camps. Well, you know, that word gets out and men put up an awful haul about that, and I know that in the camps as well as in some of the boarding houses, when the meat got tainted to where it would actually smell bad, well then the cook would throw in a bunch of saltpeter in the pot, you know, to kill that, and of course when you put a piece of meat on your plate, that meat was as red as it could be from that saltpeter. Whenever you get a piece of red meat after it's cooked, well that's saltpeter. And men would have dysentery and they'd be lined up there in a big line, you know, waiting to get into the john and running in every direction as far as that was concerned. So their food was, in some cases, pretty bad. Well that's one of the things that the IWWs was trying to eliminate. They wanted good food and they wanted clean beds, they didn't object to hard work, they didn't object to working in the mud or in the deep snow, or in the cold or in the heat. They didn't object to any of that, but they wanted, when they come in, they wanted a good meal and a clean bed, and that's what they fought for. So, you know, there wasn't anything wrong with that. But at the time,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I was asking for all these things, well then, they were radical men, see. Anybody that would ask for stuff like anything like that are radical. They're Bolsheviks, you know, they would picture them in cartoons in the newspaper with big horn -rimmed glasses and chin whiskers and a bomb in their hand, you know. That's the way the cartoonists would picture them, you know. Well, at the same time, that is the same period of time, the farmers around the country, there used to be an awful lot of smut in their wheat. Boy, I've seen blower on some of these thrash machines where it was just, you'd think that they were burning coal, there was a straw coming out, it was just a black cloud, it was smut. So every once in a while, there'd be a thrash machine that would explode and burn, well the IWWs done that, see. Well the IWWs didn't have anything to do with them farmers, they were up here in the woods. But nevertheless, in all the newspapers, if you go back into the archives of some of these newspapers, you'll see where this thrash machine, that wheat field burned, and IWWs done that. And this locomotive jumped the track, and IWWs done that. They got blamed for an awful lot of stuff that I don't think had anything to do with at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the representatives, from what you've heard, of the IWWs in the woods, were they just, in a lot of cases, a lot with Jack?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, in all cases. They were men that, well, payday comes, most of the men would head for Spokane for the weekend, and get drunk and spend all their money and come back to Camp Broke. But, while they were in Spokane, or in Seattle, or in Portland, or wherever they might be, around the lumber industry, why, some of them were picked out as an individual by organized labor, to try to organize the men in the camp. Well, you know, organized labor, for a good many years before that period, sure had a rough road to hoe in the Midwest and in the East, around the steel mills and the coal mines and stuff. You know, it developed into a civil war back into that part of the country, you know, between laborers and management. And, of course, some of that, those representatives got out into this part of the country, and of course, at that time, lumbering was a pretty big thing all through Wisconsin and Minnesota, and then jumped way out here. But, it started out, it'd be just some guy probably pulling a crosscut saw in the woods, and he'd talk to his partners, and probably convert him, and then those two would talk to two others, and so on. And, pretty soon, why, they got together to where they began to make some demands that they was either going to get some clean beds, or they was going to strike, and they were going to tie up this camp to where you can't move along.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So, what did they do? The management calls on the sheriff.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So, what does the sheriff do? Well, he organizes a home guard. He deputizes everybody that he can get a hold of. And, in some cases, they issued a whole bunch of 30 -30 rifles, and boy, they was going to, if the Wobblies come around here, well then, they was going to shoot them, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, that's the way organized labor got started. Well, in the camps, I've heard that there were strikes, or at least one strike, in most of the lumber camps out in the woods around here, that the work did stop. Oh, yeah, there was undoubtedly several work stoppages, and, of course, they wouldn't stop very long until they'd get them going again, and when they did, they had clean beds, and they had better working conditions. And then, another thing, too, is they're responsible for, I think, for the eight -hour day, you know, along with other organized labor, because ten hours was a standard work day all over in any industry, as far as that was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you heard, know about the bullpen they had in law school for the IWWs?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'd heard of it, I didn't see it, but I don't think they ever put anybody in it.
